Name: Stellar Cognition Interface
ShortDefinition: AI system that explains the life behind observed starlight.
AIEnhancement: Turns star data into explanation, placement, and learning journey.
UserFacingExplanation: This tool helps users understand what a star is, where it belongs, and what it may become.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Do not present inference as direct observation.
ConfidenceRule: Require evidence label for every generated claim.
DataSourceSuggestion: Internal concept; astronomy catalogs for real data

Name: Life Behind the Light
ShortDefinition: Central phrase for explaining the product value.
AIEnhancement: Creates emotional and scientific framing.
UserFacingExplanation: A telescope shows light; this system explains the life behind that light.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Keep poetic language scientifically grounded.
ConfidenceRule: No confidence score needed for brand copy.

Name: Telescope Image Upload
ShortDefinition: User uploads an image captured through a telescope.
AIEnhancement: AI checks image quality and suggests likely star matches.
UserFacingExplanation: Upload a telescope image to begin identifying stars in the field.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Image-based identification may be uncertain without location and time.
ConfidenceRule: Higher confidence with timestamp, location, and clear image quality.

Name: Phone Sky View
ShortDefinition: Phone camera or manual sky view used for guided observation.
AIEnhancement: AI combines image, time, and location to suggest visible stars.
UserFacingExplanation: Use your phone sky view as a starting point for star identification.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Phone images may not capture true star color or brightness.
ConfidenceRule: Mark results tentative unless catalog-matched.

Name: Catalog Search
ShortDefinition: User searches a known star or catalog object.
AIEnhancement: AI retrieves properties and explains H-R placement.
UserFacingExplanation: Search for a known star and generate its profile.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Catalog values should be cited or sourced.
ConfidenceRule: Higher confidence when multiple catalog fields agree.

Name: Right Ascension
ShortDefinition: Celestial longitude coordinate used to locate stars.
AIEnhancement: Supports catalog matching and sky position.
UserFacingExplanation: Right ascension helps locate the star on the celestial sphere.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Coordinate epoch should be noted when available.
ConfidenceRule: Confidence depends on catalog source quality.

Name: Declination
ShortDefinition: Celestial latitude coordinate used to locate stars.
AIEnhancement: Supports catalog matching and sky position.
UserFacingExplanation: Declination helps locate the star above or below the celestial equator.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Coordinate epoch should be noted when available.
ConfidenceRule: Confidence depends on catalog source quality.

Name: Apparent Magnitude
ShortDefinition: How bright a star appears from Earth.
AIEnhancement: AI compares visible brightness with distance and luminosity.
UserFacingExplanation: Apparent magnitude is how bright the star looks from Earth.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Not the same as true luminosity.
ConfidenceRule: Use with distance or parallax for stronger interpretation.

Name: Absolute Magnitude
ShortDefinition: How bright a star would appear at a standard distance of 10 parsecs.
AIEnhancement: AI uses it to estimate luminosity placement.
UserFacingExplanation: Absolute magnitude helps compare stars fairly by removing distance effects.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Requires reliable distance estimate.
ConfidenceRule: Confidence depends on parallax accuracy.

Name: Color Index
ShortDefinition: Numeric measure of star color often used to infer temperature.
AIEnhancement: AI uses color index to estimate surface temperature.
UserFacingExplanation: Color index helps show whether a star is hotter or cooler.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Dust reddening can distort color interpretation.
ConfidenceRule: Confidence improves with corrected photometry.

Name: Surface Temperature
ShortDefinition: Approximate temperature of the star surface in Kelvin.
ColorCue: Blue is hotter; red is cooler
AIEnhancement: AI places stars along the temperature axis.
UserFacingExplanation: Temperature helps determine where the star sits left to right on the H-R diagram.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Temperature estimates may vary by method.
ConfidenceRule: Use spectral class and color index together.

Name: Luminosity
ShortDefinition: Total energy output relative to the Sun.
AIEnhancement: AI places stars vertically by energy output.
UserFacingExplanation: Luminosity shows how much energy the star truly emits.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Requires distance and brightness correction.
ConfidenceRule: Higher confidence with reliable parallax.

Name: Radius
ShortDefinition: Approximate size of the star relative to the Sun.
AIEnhancement: AI explains why giants are luminous despite cooler surfaces.
UserFacingExplanation: Radius helps explain why some cool stars can still be extremely bright.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Often model-derived rather than directly measured.
ConfidenceRule: Label as inferred unless directly measured.

Name: Mass
ShortDefinition: Approximate stellar mass relative to the Sun.
AIEnhancement: AI predicts lifespan and final remnant pathway.
UserFacingExplanation: Mass strongly shapes how fast a star lives and how it dies.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Mass may be estimated indirectly.
ConfidenceRule: Use mass ranges, not false precision, when uncertain.

Name: Metallicity
ShortDefinition: Abundance of elements heavier than helium.
AIEnhancement: AI discusses age, formation history, and planet formation context.
UserFacingExplanation: Metallicity gives clues about a star's formation environment.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Often unavailable for casual observations.
ConfidenceRule: Mark missing or unknown when absent.

Name: O-Type Main Sequence
ShortDefinition: Very hot, massive, blue main sequence star.
AIEnhancement: AI explains short lifespan and intense radiation.
UserFacingExplanation: These stars burn hot and fast, living much shorter lives than Sun-like stars.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Avoid implying long-term habitability is likely.
ConfidenceRule: High confidence requires spectral class or strong catalog match.

Name: B-Type Main Sequence
ShortDefinition: Hot blue-white main sequence star.
AIEnhancement: AI explains high energy output and shorter stellar lifetime.
UserFacingExplanation: B-type stars are hot, bright, and much more massive than the Sun.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Planetary stability may be limited by lifespan and radiation.
ConfidenceRule: Use catalog spectral class for confidence.

Name: A-Type Main Sequence
ShortDefinition: White or blue-white star hotter than the Sun.
AIEnhancement: AI compares stars like Vega or Sirius A to the Sun.
UserFacingExplanation: A-type stars are bright, hot, and often visually striking in the night sky.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Do not equate visible brightness with closeness without distance data.
ConfidenceRule: Confidence improves with known spectral class.

Name: F-Type Main Sequence
ShortDefinition: Yellow-white star slightly hotter than the Sun.
AIEnhancement: AI explains moderate stability and higher UV than Sun-like stars.
UserFacingExplanation: F-type stars are hotter and brighter than the Sun, with shorter stable lifetimes.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Habitability claims require exoplanet evidence.
ConfidenceRule: Use cautious language for planetary context.

Name: G-Type Main Sequence
ShortDefinition: Sun-like yellow-white main sequence star.
AIEnhancement: AI uses the Sun as a reference point for learners.
UserFacingExplanation: G-type stars include the Sun and can provide long stable energy periods.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Do not imply every G star has habitable planets.
ConfidenceRule: Pair with planet data before habitability claims.

Name: K-Type Main Sequence
ShortDefinition: Orange main sequence star cooler than the Sun.
AIEnhancement: AI explains long lifespan and stable energy potential.
UserFacingExplanation: K-type stars are cooler, dimmer, and longer-lived than the Sun.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Flare activity varies by star and age.
ConfidenceRule: Do not generalize habitability without star-specific data.

Name: M-Type Red Dwarf
ShortDefinition: Cool, faint, long-lived main sequence star.
AIEnhancement: AI explains flare risk, tidal locking, and long lifespan.
UserFacingExplanation: Red dwarfs are small, cool, faint, and extremely long-lived.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Habitability is complex due to flares and tidal locking.
ConfidenceRule: Use exoplanet data when discussing worlds.

Name: Red Giant
ShortDefinition: Expanded late-stage star with cool surface but high luminosity.
AIEnhancement: AI shows why expanded radius increases luminosity.
UserFacingExplanation: A red giant is an aging star whose outer layers have expanded.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Do not call all red stars red giants.
ConfidenceRule: Require luminosity class or H-R placement evidence.

Name: Red Supergiant
ShortDefinition: Massive evolved star with enormous radius and high luminosity.
AIEnhancement: AI explains future supernova possibility for massive examples.
UserFacingExplanation: A red supergiant is a huge evolved star nearing an unstable late phase.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Not every red supergiant will explode on human timescales.
ConfidenceRule: Avoid exact death timing unless sourced.

Name: Blue Supergiant
ShortDefinition: Very hot, massive, extremely luminous evolved star.
AIEnhancement: AI explains mass loss, instability, and intense radiation.
UserFacingExplanation: Blue supergiants are massive, hot, and extremely luminous evolved stars.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Evolutionary paths are model-dependent.
ConfidenceRule: Use broad pathway language.

Name: White Dwarf
ShortDefinition: Hot, dense stellar remnant with low luminosity.
AIEnhancement: AI explains cooling remnant and previous red giant phase.
UserFacingExplanation: A white dwarf is the exposed remnant core of a low- or medium-mass star.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Not actively fusing like a main sequence star.
ConfidenceRule: Use remnant language, not living star language.

Name: Molecular Cloud
ShortDefinition: Cold gas and dust region where stars may form.
AIEnhancement: AI explains gravity-driven collapse and star birth.
UserFacingExplanation: Stars are born from dense regions of gas and dust.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Not a star yet; do not place as normal H-R point.
ConfidenceRule: Mark as pre-stellar stage.

Name: Protostar
ShortDefinition: Young forming star before stable hydrogen fusion.
AIEnhancement: AI explains contraction, heating, and disk formation.
UserFacingExplanation: A protostar is a forming star still gathering mass.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Properties are uncertain and often obscured by dust.
ConfidenceRule: Use pre-main sequence label.

Name: Hydrogen Fusion
ShortDefinition: Main energy process for main sequence stars.
AIEnhancement: AI explains balance between gravity and outward pressure.
UserFacingExplanation: Hydrogen fusion powers main sequence stars and keeps them stable.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Simplify without claiming all fusion stages are identical.
ConfidenceRule: Confidence tied to lifecycle classification.

Name: Core Hydrogen Depletion
ShortDefinition: Stage when core hydrogen fuel runs low.
AIEnhancement: AI shows why a star leaves the main sequence.
UserFacingExplanation: When core hydrogen runs low, the star begins changing structure.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Timescale depends strongly on mass.
ConfidenceRule: Use modeled ranges, not exact dates.

Name: Helium Fusion
ShortDefinition: Fusion of helium into heavier elements in evolved stars.
AIEnhancement: AI connects stellar aging to carbon and oxygen production.
UserFacingExplanation: Helium fusion helps create heavier elements inside evolved stars.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Element pathways vary by stellar mass.
ConfidenceRule: Use broad descriptions unless star-specific data exists.

Name: Supernova Pathway
ShortDefinition: Explosive death of some massive stars.
AIEnhancement: AI explains element dispersal and remnant formation.
UserFacingExplanation: Some massive stars end in supernovae that seed space with heavy elements.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Do not predict exact supernova timing casually.
ConfidenceRule: Requires mass and evolutionary evidence.

Name: Neutron Star
ShortDefinition: Dense remnant from some massive star explosions.
AIEnhancement: AI explains collapse, density, and pulsars when relevant.
UserFacingExplanation: A neutron star is an extremely dense remnant left by some supernovae.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Not plotted like a normal luminous star in basic H-R view.
ConfidenceRule: Use special remnant layer.

Name: Black Hole
ShortDefinition: Collapsed remnant where gravity prevents light from escaping.
AIEnhancement: AI explains endpoint for very massive stars.
UserFacingExplanation: A black hole can form when a very massive stellar core collapses.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Do not treat as directly visible on H-R diagram.
ConfidenceRule: Represent as lifecycle endpoint, not H-R point.

Name: Habitable Zone
ShortDefinition: Region around a star where liquid water could exist on a planet surface under right conditions.
AIEnhancement: AI estimates zone conceptually based on luminosity.
UserFacingExplanation: The habitable zone depends on stellar luminosity and planetary atmosphere.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Habitable zone does not guarantee life.
ConfidenceRule: Require planet data for planet-specific claims.

Name: Stellar Flares
ShortDefinition: Sudden bursts of radiation from magnetically active stars.
AIEnhancement: AI flags flare activity as atmospheric and biological risk factor.
UserFacingExplanation: Flares can affect planetary atmospheres and surface conditions.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Do not overgeneralize across all red dwarfs.
ConfidenceRule: Use activity indicators when available.

Name: Tidal Locking
ShortDefinition: Planet keeps one side facing its star due to orbital locking.
AIEnhancement: AI explains climate implications for close-in planets.
UserFacingExplanation: Close-in planets around cool stars may become tidally locked.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Tidal locking does not automatically make a planet uninhabitable.
ConfidenceRule: Use cautious phrasing.

Name: Hydrogen
ShortDefinition: Most abundant element and main fuel for stars.
AIEnhancement: AI connects hydrogen fusion to stellar energy.
UserFacingExplanation: Hydrogen is the primary fuel of main sequence stars.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Avoid implying stars created most primordial hydrogen.
ConfidenceRule: Mark as primordial fuel source.

Name: Helium
ShortDefinition: Produced by hydrogen fusion in stars and also formed early in the universe.
AIEnhancement: AI connects helium production to stellar fusion.
UserFacingExplanation: Stars create helium by fusing hydrogen in their cores.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Some helium is primordial from the early universe.
ConfidenceRule: Separate stellar and primordial origins.

Name: Carbon
ShortDefinition: Key life-related element formed in evolved stars.
AIEnhancement: AI connects red giant evolution to carbon production.
UserFacingExplanation: Carbon is produced in evolved stars and becomes part of future planets and life chemistry.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Production depends on stellar mass and stage.
ConfidenceRule: Use broad element legacy language.

Name: Oxygen
ShortDefinition: Major element formed in massive stars and distributed by stellar processes.
AIEnhancement: AI explains oxygen as part of cosmic chemical inheritance.
UserFacingExplanation: Oxygen is forged in stars and later becomes part of planets, water, and atmospheres.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Distribution pathway depends on stellar death and mass loss.
ConfidenceRule: Avoid simplistic one-source claims.

Name: Iron
ShortDefinition: Element formed in late-stage massive stars before core collapse.
AIEnhancement: AI explains why iron is tied to massive star evolution.
UserFacingExplanation: Iron is associated with late fusion stages in massive stars and supernova events.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Do not imply low-mass stars create iron cores.
ConfidenceRule: Require massive-star context.

Name: Gold
ShortDefinition: Heavy element associated with neutron star mergers and some explosive processes.
AIEnhancement: AI connects heavy elements to extreme cosmic events.
UserFacingExplanation: Gold is linked to rare, extreme events such as neutron star mergers.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Avoid claiming every supernova creates gold in large amounts.
ConfidenceRule: Use modern heavy-element origin nuance.

Name: Student Mode
ShortDefinition: Simplified explanations for learners.
AIEnhancement: AI reduces complexity while staying accurate.
UserFacingExplanation: This mode explains stars in clear, beginner-friendly language.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Do not remove scientific truth for simplicity.
ConfidenceRule: No confidence change; explanation style only.

Name: Research Mode
ShortDefinition: Detailed technical explanations with evidence and uncertainty.
AIEnhancement: AI includes catalog data, uncertainty, and technical terms.
UserFacingExplanation: This mode gives deeper technical detail for advanced users.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Must show evidence and limitations.
ConfidenceRule: Require source and confidence fields.

Name: Public Wonder Mode
ShortDefinition: Emotionally resonant but accurate explanation style.
AIEnhancement: AI uses wonder language grounded in science.
UserFacingExplanation: This mode helps the public feel the meaning of what they are seeing.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Do not invent facts for drama.
ConfidenceRule: Confidence applies to science claims, not tone.

Name: NASA STEM Outreach Mode
ShortDefinition: Mission-aligned educational explanation for workshops and demos.
AIEnhancement: AI connects astronomy, data literacy, AI, and scientific reasoning.
UserFacingExplanation: This mode frames star observation as a STEM learning experience.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Avoid using official endorsement unless granted.
ConfidenceRule: Require neutral institutional language.

Name: Evidence Trail
ShortDefinition: Visible record of data used for each AI conclusion.
AIEnhancement: AI shows source fields and why they matter.
UserFacingExplanation: The evidence trail shows why the system made a classification.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Must distinguish sources, estimates, and missing data.
ConfidenceRule: Classification must reference at least one evidence item.

Name: Confidence Score
ShortDefinition: Numeric or labeled estimate of reliability.
AIEnhancement: AI communicates uncertainty clearly.
UserFacingExplanation: Confidence shows how strongly the available data supports the result.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Confidence is not truth; it is evidence strength.
ConfidenceRule: Use low, medium, high or percent with rules.

Name: Uncertainty Halo
ShortDefinition: Visual radius around H-R placement showing possible error.
AIEnhancement: AI displays possible placement range instead of a single false-certain dot.
UserFacingExplanation: A wider halo means the star's placement is less certain.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Do not hide uncertainty for cleaner visuals.
ConfidenceRule: Halo size tied to data completeness and measurement error.

Name: Stellar Report Generator
ShortDefinition: Creates a saved report after an observation session.
AIEnhancement: AI summarizes star profile, H-R placement, evidence, and takeaway.
UserFacingExplanation: Generate a report that explains what you observed and what it means.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Report must label inferred and simulated values.
ConfidenceRule: Require source and timestamp in report.

Name: STEM Challenge Mode
ShortDefinition: Guided learning tasks based on star types and H-R regions.
AIEnhancement: AI recommends challenges and checks user understanding.
UserFacingExplanation: Complete missions like finding a main sequence star or comparing a red giant to the Sun.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Challenges should teach, not overstate ability to observe faint objects.
ConfidenceRule: Completion based on app interaction, not scientific discovery.

Name: AI Telescope Coach
ShortDefinition: AI-guided observation quality support.
AIEnhancement: AI suggests focus, exposure, target timing, and light pollution adjustments.
UserFacingExplanation: The coach helps improve your image and observation conditions.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Image advice should be framed as guidance, not guaranteed fix.
ConfidenceRule: Confidence depends on image metadata and visible quality.

Name: Interactive H-R Diagram
ShortDefinition: Main visual chart showing stars by temperature and luminosity.
AIEnhancement: AI places and explains stars on the chart.
UserFacingExplanation: The H-R diagram shows how star temperature and luminosity reveal lifecycle stage.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Axes must be clearly labeled and orientation explained.
ConfidenceRule: Placement confidence must appear near plotted point.

Name: Star Biography Card
ShortDefinition: Profile panel summarizing a selected star.
AIEnhancement: AI generates readable summary from structured fields.
UserFacingExplanation: The biography card summarizes the star's identity, properties, stage, and meaning.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Separate known facts from generated explanation.
ConfidenceRule: Require last-updated and source fields.

Name: Lifecycle Timeline
ShortDefinition: Timeline view of possible past and future star stages.
AIEnhancement: AI maps star mass/type to likely pathway.
UserFacingExplanation: The timeline shows how this type of star may change over cosmic time.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Future path is model-based, not directly observed.
ConfidenceRule: Confidence depends on mass and stage data.

Name: Element Overlay
ShortDefinition: Overlay linking star stages to element creation.
AIEnhancement: AI connects stellar evolution to chemical elements.
UserFacingExplanation: The overlay shows how stars help create and distribute elements.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Element creation should be simplified carefully.
ConfidenceRule: Use broad pathways unless detailed nucleosynthesis data is available.

Name: ObservationSessions Table
ShortDefinition: Stores each user viewing or analysis session.
AIEnhancement: Enables reports, progress tracking, and saved observations.
UserFacingExplanation: Each session records what the user observed and when.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Avoid storing precise location unless user consents.
ConfidenceRule: SessionID should be unique.

Name: Stars Table
ShortDefinition: Stores structured star properties.
AIEnhancement: Supports reusable star profiles and H-R placement.
UserFacingExplanation: The Stars table stores scientific properties used by the app.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Keep source and update date for each record.
ConfidenceRule: StarID should be unique.

Name: AIExplanations Table
ShortDefinition: Stores generated explanations and mode used.
AIEnhancement: Preserves AI outputs for review and reporting.
UserFacingExplanation: The app stores explanations so users can revisit or export them.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Generated text should include timestamp and model/version if available.
ConfidenceRule: ExplanationID should be unique.

Name: HRPlacements Table
ShortDefinition: Stores plotted H-R diagram positions and confidence.
AIEnhancement: Separates chart placement from raw star data.
UserFacingExplanation: This table stores where each star appears on the diagram.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Placement should include confidence and evidence fields.
ConfidenceRule: PlacementID should be unique.

Name: EvidenceRecords Table
ShortDefinition: Stores data points supporting AI conclusions.
AIEnhancement: Enables transparent reasoning and auditability.
UserFacingExplanation: Each evidence record explains one reason behind the AI result.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Evidence should map to a source and claim.
ConfidenceRule: EvidenceID should be unique.

Name: Generate Stellar Report Flow
ShortDefinition: Power Automate flow that creates a report after a session.
AIEnhancement: AI summarizes session and writes a report record.
UserFacingExplanation: After a session, generate a clear report of the observation and learning takeaway.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Report should state when values are estimated.
ConfidenceRule: Run only after required fields are present.

Name: Send Mentor Summary Flow
ShortDefinition: Optional flow that emails a report to a teacher or mentor.
AIEnhancement: AI formats a short summary for review.
UserFacingExplanation: Send a polished summary to an educator, mentor, or program lead.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Only send with user permission.
ConfidenceRule: Require consent before email action.

Name: Observed vs Inferred Label
ShortDefinition: Required label showing how each value was obtained.
AIEnhancement: AI tags each field by evidence type.
UserFacingExplanation: Every value should say whether it was observed, catalog-based, inferred, or simulated.
ScientificIntegrityNote: This is required to prevent scientific overclaiming.
ConfidenceRule: Missing label reduces confidence.

Name: Missing Data Warning
ShortDefinition: Warning shown when needed fields are unavailable.
AIEnhancement: AI explains what cannot be concluded yet.
UserFacingExplanation: The app will tell users what data is missing and why it matters.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Never fill missing data with invented precision.
ConfidenceRule: Missing key fields lower confidence.

Name: Alternative Classification
ShortDefinition: Secondary possible classification when evidence is incomplete.
AIEnhancement: AI shows plausible alternatives instead of one forced answer.
UserFacingExplanation: When data is limited, the app can show another possible classification.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Alternative should be clearly labeled as less likely.
ConfidenceRule: Use only when ambiguity is meaningful.

Name: Find the Main Sequence
ShortDefinition: User identifies or searches a main sequence star.
AIEnhancement: AI checks answer and explains placement.
UserFacingExplanation: Find a star that belongs on the main sequence and explain why.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Use known catalog examples for beginner success.
ConfidenceRule: Challenge confidence based on selected star type.

Name: Compare to the Sun
ShortDefinition: User compares another star with the Sun.
AIEnhancement: AI explains differences in temperature, luminosity, mass, and lifespan.
UserFacingExplanation: Compare a selected star to the Sun to understand scale and lifecycle.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Solar values should be presented as reference values.
ConfidenceRule: Use trusted reference values.

Name: Trace a Star Lifecycle
ShortDefinition: User explores how a star changes over time.
AIEnhancement: AI guides lifecycle pathway and checks understanding.
UserFacingExplanation: Trace how a star moves through the H-R diagram as it ages.
ScientificIntegrityNote: Lifecycle paths are model-based simplifications.
ConfidenceRule: Confidence depends on mass range and class.
